name: Barista Competition Programme
purpose: Creates a structured programme that uses barista competitions — both internal and external — as a development tool for advancing technical skills, building team motivation, raising the cafe's industry profile, and creating a culture of excellence. This block covers internal throwdowns, preparation for regional and national SCA competitions, budgeting for competition entry, and leveraging competition success for marketing and recruitment.

detailed_objectives: Establish regular internal barista throwdowns as a fun, low-stakes development activity
Identify and prepare team members who aspire to compete in external SCA-sanctioned competitions
Develop competition-grade skills in espresso, milk texturing, latte art, and signature drinks
Build a competition budget covering entry fees, practice coffee, travel, and equipment
Leverage competition participation and results for brand positioning and recruitment marketing
secondary_objectives: Create a pipeline of highly skilled baristas whose competition experience elevates daily service quality
Connect the cafe to the broader specialty coffee community through competition networks
Boost team morale and pride through shared competitive experiences and celebrations of achievement
Attract ambitious baristas who seek employers that support their competitive ambitions

why_this_matters: Barista competitions are the most effective accelerator of technical skill development in the coffee industry. The preparation process demands a level of precision, consistency, and understanding that far exceeds what daily cafe service requires — and that is precisely the point. A barista preparing for a latte art competition practises hundreds of pours, developing muscle memory and milk texturing intuition that makes their daily service flawless. One preparing for an espresso competition studies extraction theory, water chemistry, and sensory evaluation at a depth that transforms how they approach every shot they pull. The skills developed in competition practice cascade directly into better coffee quality, faster diagnosis of problems, and more confident customer engagement. Beyond individual development, competitions build team culture. Internal throwdowns — informal competitions where team members compete in latte art, blind tasting, or speed challenges — create energy, camaraderie, and healthy rivalry. They give quieter team members a chance to shine and reveal hidden talent. External competitions connect the cafe to the wider specialty coffee community, building relationships with other baristas, roasters, and equipment suppliers that bring knowledge and opportunities back to the business. From a commercial perspective, competition participation is powerful marketing. A cafe that can say its barista placed in the UK Latte Art Championship or competed at the regional brewing competition has a credibility claim that no amount of advertising can replicate. It signals to customers that this cafe takes its craft seriously. It signals to prospective employees that this is a place where talent is developed, not just exploited. The investment is modest — entry fees, practice coffee, and occasional travel — relative to the return in skills, culture, and brand value.

application_logic.L1: IMMEDIATE (0-2 weeks). Week 1: Start with internal throwdowns. These require almost no budget and create immediate engagement. Organise the first throwdown as a latte art competition — it is the most visual, accessible, and fun category. Rules: each barista pours three drinks, the team votes on the best pour (or invite a guest judge for impartiality), and the winner gets a small prize (a bag of specialty coffee, a voucher, or simply bragging rights). Schedule throwdowns monthly — alternate between latte art, espresso accuracy (closest to a target recipe), blind tasting, and speed challenges. Week 2: Research the external competition calendar. The UK Barista Championship, UK Brewers Cup, UK Latte Art Championship, and UK Coffee in Good Spirits Championship are all sanctioned by the SCA and run through regional heats. Identify the nearest regional heat, its date, entry requirements, and fees. Share the information with the team — gauge interest without pressure. If someone expresses interest, begin supporting them with dedicated practice time and mentorship from the head barista.
application_logic.L2: SYSTEM BUILD (2-8 weeks). Formalise the competition programme. Create a Competition Programme Policy covering: how internal throwdowns are scheduled and judged, how external competition candidates are identified and supported, what financial support the cafe provides (entry fees, practice coffee, travel expenses), and what time commitment is expected from the competitor (practice sessions during or outside work hours). For external competitors, develop a Preparation Plan. SCA competitions typically require: a signature drink (an original recipe showcasing creativity), a technical espresso service (consistency, workflow, time management), and a presentation to judges (communication, knowledge, engagement). Practice should begin at least 8 weeks before the competition date, with weekly sessions building toward the full routine. Source practice coffee from the roaster — many roasters will sponsor competition coffee in exchange for being credited at the event. Build a Competition Kit: the competitor needs their preferred equipment (grinder, tamper, distribution tool, milk pitcher, cups) tuned and familiar. Document the preparation process — it becomes training material for future competitors.
application_logic.L3: CULTURE & EXCELLENCE (8+ weeks). Competition culture should now be embedded in the cafe's identity. Support at least one external competition entry per year from the team. Celebrate participation regardless of placement — competing is the achievement, not just winning. Use competition stories in marketing: social media posts about preparation, behind-the-scenes practice videos, competition day coverage, and results announcements. Invite the competitor to present their experience to the team after each event — what they learned, what they would change, what skills they brought back. Build relationships with other competing cafes — the competition circuit is a tight community where alliances lead to guest shifts, collaborative events, and knowledge sharing. Host a public throwdown or competition viewing event at the cafe to engage customers in the competitive coffee culture. Develop a competition alumni track record — over years, this becomes a powerful recruitment and marketing asset. Consider sponsoring or hosting a local throwdown event, positioning the cafe as a hub for coffee excellence in the area.

L1_setup.title: Immediate Actions
actions: Organise the first internal latte art throwdown with simple rules and a small prize
Schedule monthly throwdowns alternating between latte art, espresso, tasting, and speed categories
Research the external SCA competition calendar for the current year
Share competition information with the team and gauge interest in external entry
Identify any team members with competition potential or aspiration
Set aside a small budget for throwdown prizes and external competition exploration
Document throwdown results and celebrate winners on social media

L2_build.title: System Development
actions: Create a Competition Programme Policy covering internal and external support
Develop an External Competition Preparation Plan template with 8-week timeline
Establish financial support terms: entry fees, practice coffee, travel, and equipment
Source practice coffee from the roaster partner, negotiating sponsorship if possible
Build a Competition Kit checklist of all equipment and supplies needed
Schedule dedicated practice sessions for external competitors within the team rota
Document preparation processes as training material for future competitors

L3_embed.title: Culture Embedding
actions: Support at least one external competition entry per year from the team
Celebrate participation and share competition stories across marketing channels
Invite competitors to present their experience and learnings to the full team
Build relationships with other competing cafes and the broader competition community
Host a public throwdown or competition viewing event at the cafe
Develop a competition alumni record as a recruitment and marketing asset

tools.description: Planning template for monthly throwdowns: competition category, rules, judging criteria, equipment needed, prize, and results recording. Includes latte art, espresso accuracy, blind tasting, and speed challenge formats.
8-week structured preparation plan for SCA-sanctioned competitions: weekly milestones for signature drink development, technical routine practice, presentation rehearsal, and equipment preparation.

Policy document defining how the cafe supports competitions: selection criteria for external entry, financial support terms, practice time allocation, equipment provision, and post-competition knowledge sharing requirements.
Budget template covering all competition costs: entry fees, practice coffee, equipment, travel, accommodation, and incidentals, with annual budget allocation and per-competition cost tracking.
Historical log of all internal and external competitions: date, category, participants, results, key learnings, skills brought back to the cafe, and marketing content generated from each event.

failure_modes: Throwdowns become stale or poorly attended because they are not varied enough or prizes are not motivating
External competition preparation consumes disproportionate management attention while daily operations suffer
Only one or two team members are included in competition culture while others feel excluded or uninspired
Competition investment is not tracked against returns, making it difficult to justify ongoing budget allocation
kpis: Monthly internal throwdowns maintained with at least 75% of eligible team members participating
At least one team member entering an external SCA-sanctioned competition per year
Measurable improvement in team latte art and espresso consistency scores following throwdown practice
Competition-related social media content generating above-average engagement compared to standard posts
constraints: External competition entry fees, travel, and practice coffee represent a real cost that must be budgeted
Competition preparation requires dedicated practice time that must be balanced against operational needs
SCA competition calendars are fixed — preparation timelines must work backward from published dates

time_load.L1: 2 hours (first throwdown organisation, competition calendar research)
time_load.L2: 8 hours (policy creation, preparation plan development, budget planning, practice session scheduling)
time_load.L3: 4 hours/quarter (competition support, marketing content, community engagement)

review_protocol: Monthly throwdown debrief with team. Post-competition review session for external entries. Quarterly competition programme review by owner and head barista. Annual budget and programme strategy review.

meta.summary_for_humans: This block uses barista competitions — from fun internal throwdowns to serious external SCA entries — as a powerful development tool. Competition practice accelerates skills, builds team culture, raises the cafe's profile, and attracts ambitious talent.
implementation_risks: Throwdowns must stay fun and inclusive — if they become too serious or exclusive, they damage rather than build team culture
External competition failure can be demoralising — frame every entry as a learning experience, not just a podium attempt
Competition participation must not become a substitute for systematic daily training — it is a complement, not a replacement
meta.notes_for_engine: Activate when signals indicate team members expressing competitive ambition, desire to raise coffee standards, or need for team engagement activities. Requires B197 (Coffee Education) and B068 (staff development) as foundations.
